title: 1.6 Tenant Guards & Deduplication System
lecture:
1. Conceptual Breakdown (The "What" and "Why")

Email deliverability is a fragile asset. If an automated system sends multiple emails to the same recipient in a short window of time, the recipient will get annoyed, mark the email as spam, and domain regulators will blacklist the client's email domain. If that domain is blacklisted, 100% of all future emails go straight to the spam folder, ruining the business outreach system.

To protect our clients, we must enforce Deduplication Gates at two different levels:

  • Tenant-Level Deduplication: Restricts outreach so a specific client (tenant) cannot contact the same prospect email multiple times, even if that email appears in different CSV lists they upload.
  • Global Block List (Opt-Out): If a prospect clicks 'unsubscribe' or asks to be removed, we flag them with a global_block = 1 in our central database. This blocks **all** clients in our system from emailing them, protecting the entire system from compliance violations (like CAN-SPAM laws).

By saving this record in a relational database with strict constraints, we check and intercept duplicates at the speed of milliseconds before any code calls external email delivery APIs.

2. Code-Level & File Walkthrough

The deduplication logic is managed in [leadGuard.ts](file:///Users/ashleybutler/ai-funnel-machine/src/outbound/leadGuard.ts). Let's look at the database setup and queries:

CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS lead_registry (
  id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY AUTOINCREMENT,
  email TEXT NOT NULL,
  client_id TEXT NOT NULL,
  global_block INTEGER DEFAULT 0,
  processed_at TEXT NOT NULL,
  UNIQUE(email, client_id)
);

CREATE INDEX IF NOT EXISTS idx_leads_lookup ON lead_registry(email, client_id);

Line-by-Line Breakdown:

  • UNIQUE(email, client_id): This constraint creates a database-level lock. It guarantees that the same email cannot exist under the same client ID twice, providing database-level safety.
  • CREATE INDEX: Indexes speed up lookups. Instead of scanning millions of rows row-by-row, SQLite builds a fast lookup tree on the `email` and `client_id` columns.
  • isDuplicateForClient Method: The code executes the query:
    • SELECT 1 FROM lead_registry 
WHERE email = ? AND (client_id = ? OR global_block = 1) 
LIMIT 1

    This checks if the email exists for the active tenant, OR if the lead is globally blocked. If a record is returned, the system immediately returns true and blocks the outbound campaign.
exercise:
3. Step-by-Step Guided Exercise

Trigger a duplicate warning manually and verify that the database constraint blocks duplicate emails.

  • Step A: Open the file: /Users/ashleybutler/ai-funnel-machine/intake/leads_test.csv.
  • Step B: Add a second row to the CSV sheet with the exact same email address as the first row, but change the company name to verify it is evaluated separately:
    • businessName,email,firstName,lastName,niche,phone,website
"Pro Restoration","pro@example.com","John","Smith","water mitigation","555-0199","http://pro.com"
"Alt Restoration","pro@example.com","John","Smith","water mitigation","555-0199","http://pro.com"
  • Step C: Run the outbound pipeline in your terminal: npx tsx run-outbound.ts --client=default_client.
  • Step D: Look at the logs. Confirm that the first row processes and outputs `Success!`, while the second row outputs ⚠️ [TENANT GUARD] Duplicate flagged for Client! Skipping.... This confirms the system successfully caught and blocked the duplicate lead.

quizzes.question: What is the key danger of sending multiple cold outreach emails to the same recipient in a short timeframe?
options: The system runs out of database memory.
The recipient marks the email as spam, harming the sender domain's reputation and causing future emails to go to spam.
The Google Calendar API throws a rate limit error.
It compiles the code incorrectly.
quizzes.explanation: Spam complaints directly damage domain sender authority. Earning too many spam complaints will cause email providers to block your domain entirely, rendering it useless.

quizzes.question: Which database table stores the historical record of outbound leads sent by each client?
options: call_logs
lead_registry
knowledge_base
client_settings
quizzes.explanation: lead_registry serves as the central record-keeping table that tracks which email addresses have been registered, processed, or globally blocked.

quizzes.question: What does a global_block value of 1 indicate in a lead registry row?
options: The lead is ready to be sent a Google Calendar link.
The lead is unsubscribed/blocked globally, and no client in the system is permitted to contact them.
The database connection has timed out.
The lead's phone number is verified.
quizzes.explanation: global_block = 1 represents an opt-out flag. Any queries checking duplication return true if this flag is set, blocking outreach across all tenants.

quizzes.question: Why did we create the database index 'idx_leads_lookup'?
options: To encrypt the email strings before writing to SQLite.
To speed up lead searches, preventing slow database scans as the lead volume grows over time.
To make the table read-only.
To sync data with Google Sheets.
quizzes.explanation: Indexes allow relational databases to locate target rows in logarithmic time instead of scanning every single row in the table, keeping verification times fast.

quizzes.question: In the lead_registry table, what does the UNIQUE(email, client_id) constraint guarantee?
options: That an email can only be contacted by a single client globally.
That no individual client can have the same email address registered more than once.
That all emails must end with .com.
That client_id must match a Google Calendar email.
quizzes.explanation: A composite UNIQUE constraint enforces uniqueness across the combination of columns. It blocks duplicate entries for the same client, while allowing different clients to contact the same email if needed.
